key_insight: The paper couples sparsified physics-augmented neural constitutive laws with finite-element topology optimization to jointly optimize material composition and structure in multimaterial 3D printing.
engineering_relevance: This enables end-to-end design of soft, functionally graded components such as robotic grippers, reducing reliance on trial-and-error prototyping.
method: Learn closed-form, composition-aware hyperelastic laws from experimental data.
Use exact symbolic differentiation and the adjoint state method in FEniCSx for efficient gradients.
Optimize both spatial material distribution and macroscopic topology under stretch/non-failure constraints.
evidence_signal: The framework is demonstrated on soft robotic gripper applications, showing continuous composition optimization and concurrent topology/material optimization for anisotropic contact responses.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate constitutive identification and careful implementation of differentiable FE/adjoint pipelines, which may be sensitive to data quality and model mismatch.
paper_title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing
